title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return its maximum depth. The maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root down to the farthest leaf node.
hints: Recursion: depth(node) = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)).
Empty tree ka depth 0 hai — base case sambhal le.
Iterative BFS bhi chalega — har level pe counter bump kar.

title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the inorder traversal of its node values. Inorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the current node, then the right subtree.
hints: Recursion: inorder(left) -> visit -> inorder(right).
Iterative: explicit stack — push lefts till null, pop, go right.
Empty tree -> empty array.

title: Binary Tree Preorder Traversal
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the preorder traversal of its node values. Preorder traversal visits the current node, then the left subtree, then the right subtree.
hints: Visit root, then preorder(left), then preorder(right).
Iterative: stack, push right first then left (LIFO).
Single node -> [val].

title: Binary Tree Postorder Traversal
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the postorder traversal of its node values. Postorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the right subtree, then the current node.
hints: Recursion: postorder(left) -> postorder(right) -> visit.
Iterative: two stacks or reverse-preorder trick.
Useful when child results feed into parent.

title: Count Binary Tree Nodes
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the total number of nodes in the tree.
hints: DFS: count = 1 + count(left) + count(right).
Empty -> 0.
BFS with queue size accumulation also works.

title: Sum of Binary Tree Values
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the sum of all node values.
hints: DFS aggregator.
sum = node.val + sum(left) + sum(right).
Empty subtree contributes 0.

title: Invert Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), invert it by swapping the left and right children of every node. Return the inverted tree as a level-order encoded array.
hints: Recursion: invert(left) and invert(right), then swap.
BFS works too — for each dequeued node swap children, enqueue both.
treeToArray(root) for level-order output.

title: Symmetric Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return `true` if the tree is symmetric, i.e. a mirror image of itself around its center, and `false` otherwise.
hints: Compare left subtree against mirrored right subtree.
Helper: isMirror(a, b) — both null OK, one null fail, val match + cross-recurse.
Empty tree is symmetric.

title: Same Tree
description: Given two binary trees `a` and `b`, each encoded in level-order (using `null` for missing nodes), return `true` if they are identical in both structure and node values, and `false` otherwise.
hints: Recurse both in lockstep — both null pass, one null fail.
Values must match before recursing left and right.
treeToArray is not safe — structure matters!

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return its level-order traversal grouped by level, i.e. a list where each entry contains the node values at one level, ordered from the root level downward and left to right within each level.
hints: BFS with a queue — process current level fully before the next.
Capture queue length at the start of each level iteration.
Empty tree -> empty array.

title: Search in a Binary Search Tree
description: Given a binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree` and an integer `val`, find the node whose value equals `val`. Return the level-order encoded array of the subtree rooted at that node, or `[]` if no such node exists.
hints: BST property: target < node -> go left, > -> go right.
Found -> return that subtree.
Use treeToArray on the matched node.

title: Minimum Value in BST
description: Given a non-empty binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree`, return its smallest value.
hints: Walk left until null — last node is the min in a BST.
Iterative is cleanest.
Single node -> that value.

title: Maximum Value in BST
description: Given a non-empty binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree`, return its largest value.
hints: Walk right till null — that's the max.
Iterative loop suffices.
Empty case ruled out by constraints.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return `true` if it is a valid binary search tree. A valid BST requires that for every node, all values in its left subtree are strictly less than the node's value and all values in its right subtree are strictly greater.
hints: Pass min/max bounds down — comparing only with parent is wrong.
Helper(node, lo, hi). Left -> hi=node.val. Right -> lo=node.val.
Inorder traversal must be strictly increasing — alternative.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
description: Given a binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree` and two values `p` and `q` (both guaranteed to exist in the tree), return the value of their lowest common ancestor, i.e. the deepest node that has both `p` and `q` as descendants (a node may be a descendant of itself).
hints: BST trick: walk down. If both p,q < node -> go left. Both > -> right. Else node is LCA.
Iterative O(h) without recursion.
If p == node or q == node and other is in subtree — node itself is LCA.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (not necessarily a BST) and two distinct values `p` and `q` (both guaranteed to exist), return the value of their lowest common ancestor, i.e. the deepest node that has both `p` and `q` as descendants (a node may be a descendant of itself). All node values are unique.
hints: Recursive: if root is null or matches p/q, return root.
Recurse left and right. If both come back non-null, root is LCA.
Otherwise return whichever side returned non-null.

title: Balanced Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return `true` if it is height-balanced. A tree is height-balanced if, for every node, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by at most 1.
hints: DFS returns height OR -1 (unbalanced sentinel).
If either child returns -1 or |hL - hR| > 1, propagate -1.
Avoids the n^2 trap of computing height at every node.

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return its diameter: the length, in edges, of the longest path between any two nodes. This path may or may not pass through the root.
hints: DFS returns height; track best left+right at every node.
Diameter (in edges) = max over all nodes of (heightLeft + heightRight).
Path may not pass through root.

title: Path Sum
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes) and an integer `target`, return `true` if there exists a root-to-leaf path whose node values sum to `target`, and `false` otherwise.
hints: DFS subtracting node.val; at a leaf check remaining == 0.
Empty tree -> false (no path).
Leaf = no left and no right.

title: Binary Tree Paths
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return all root-to-leaf paths, each represented as the list of node values along that path. Paths should appear in DFS preorder discovery order.
hints: DFS carrying the current path array.
Leaf: push a copy of path into output.
Backtrack: pop after recursing.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
description: Given a binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree` and an integer `k`, return the value of the kth smallest node (1-indexed).
hints: Inorder traversal of a BST gives values sorted ascending.
Stop early at the kth visit — saves work.
Iterative inorder with stack is the cleanest early-exit form.

title: Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree
description: Given an integer array `nums` sorted in ascending order, build a height-balanced binary search tree and return its level-order encoded array. At each step, choose the middle element of the current slice as the root; for an even-length slice, pick the left middle element (`floor((lo+hi)/2)`).
hints: Recursion: pick middle, build left from [lo, mid-1], right from [mid+1, hi].
Use floor((lo+hi)/2) for the middle.
Then serialize the resulting tree level-order.

title: Insert into a Binary Search Tree
description: Given a binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree` and an integer `val` (guaranteed not already present), insert `val` as a new leaf along the standard BST search path. Return the level-order encoded array of the resulting tree.
hints: Recursion: if root null create node; else descend left or right.
Iterative pointer-walk also works.
Use treeToArray to serialize.

title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), flatten it in place into a right-skewed linked list: each node's left child becomes `null` and its right child points to the next node in preorder traversal. Return the level-order encoded array of the flattened tree.
hints: Reverse postorder trick: build right -> left -> root and stitch with prev pointer.
Or do a preorder collection then re-link.
After flattening every node has left=null, right=next.

title: Binary Tree Right Side View
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the values of the nodes visible from the right side, ordered from top to bottom. The right-side view consists of the rightmost node at each level.
hints: BFS: at each level, last dequeued node is the right view.
DFS preorder, right before left, recording first hit per depth, also works.
Empty -> [].

title: Average of Levels in Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return a list of the average values of the nodes at each level, ordered from the root level downward.
hints: BFS by level — sum/count per level.
Use level size at the start of each iteration.
Floating-point division in JS is fine.

title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
description: Given two integer arrays `preorder` and `inorder` representing the preorder and inorder traversals of a binary tree with unique values, reconstruct the tree and return its level-order encoded array.
constraints: Both arrays describe the same tree
hints: First of preorder is root.
Find root in inorder — left of it is left subtree, right is right.
Use a Map<val, inorder index> for O(1) lookup.

title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (node values may be negative), return the maximum path sum. A path is any sequence of connected nodes that visits each node at most once and need not pass through the root; it must contain at least one node. The path sum is the sum of the values of the nodes it contains.
hints: DFS returning best 'gain' from below (single chain).
At each node, candidate = node.val + max(0, leftGain) + max(0, rightGain).
Track global max separately from returned chain.

title: Delete Node in a BST
description: Given a binary search tree encoded in level-order as `tree` and an integer `key`, delete the node with value `key` and return the level-order encoded array of the resulting BST. If the deleted node has two children, replace its value with its inorder successor (the smallest value in its right subtree) and delete that successor. If `key` is not present, the tree is unchanged.
hints: Locate via BST descent.
0 or 1 child: replace with the surviving child (or null).
2 children: copy successor's value into node, then delete successor in right subtree.

title: Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the number of good nodes. A node is good if no node on the path from the root to it has a value strictly greater than the node's own value.
hints: DFS carrying the maximum-so-far on the path.
If node.val >= maxSoFar, it's good; update max and recurse.
Root is always good.

title: House Robber III
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` where each node holds a non-negative value, return the maximum total value you can select subject to the constraint that no two directly connected nodes (a parent and its child) may both be selected.
hints: DFS returns pair [robThis, skipThis].
robThis = node.val + skipLeft + skipRight.
skipThis = max(robLeft, skipLeft) + max(robRight, skipRight).

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree provided as a level-order encoded array `data` (using `null` for missing nodes), build the tree and then re-serialize it back to its canonical level-order encoded array. Return that array.
hints: arrayToTree handles deserialization (already provided).
treeToArray handles serialization.
Round-tripping should yield the same canonical level-order array.

title: Boundary of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return its boundary in anti-clockwise order: the root, then the left boundary nodes top-to-bottom excluding leaves, then all leaves left-to-right, then the right boundary nodes bottom-to-top excluding leaves, without duplicating the root. If the root is itself a leaf, return `[root.val]`. The left boundary starts at `root.left` and repeatedly goes left if possible else right, including each non-leaf node visited; the right boundary starts at `root.right` and repeatedly goes right if possible else left, including each non-leaf node visited (then reversed).
hints: Three pieces: left boundary (no leaves), all leaves L-to-R, reversed right boundary (no leaves).
Always start with root; single-node tree returns [root.val].
Be careful: do not double-count root as boundary AND leaf.

title: Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return its vertical order traversal. Assign the root column index 0, a left child its parent's column minus 1, and a right child its parent's column plus 1. Output one list per column from leftmost to rightmost; within a column, order nodes by depth ascending, breaking ties (same column and depth) by value ascending.
hints: BFS or DFS recording (col, depth, val).
Group by col, sort each group by (depth, val).
Or use a Map<col, list> with BFS so depth order falls out, then sort same-(col,depth) by val.

title: Recover Binary Search Tree
description: A valid binary search tree had exactly two of its node values swapped by mistake. Given the tree encoded in level-order as `tree`, recover it by swapping those two values back, modifying the tree in place. Return the level-order encoded array of the corrected tree.
hints: Inorder of a BST is strictly increasing.
Find the two violations during inorder; swap their values.
The two swapped nodes might be adjacent (one violation) or not (two violations).

title: Count Complete Tree Nodes
description: Given a complete binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (every level is fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled left to right), return the number of nodes. Aim to exploit the completeness property to count in better than linear time, e.g. O((log n)^2).
hints: Compute leftmost depth (lh) and rightmost depth (rh).
If lh == rh, subtree is full -> 2^lh - 1.
Otherwise recurse left + recurse right + 1.

title: Maximum Width of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return its maximum width. The width of a level is the number of positions between its leftmost and rightmost non-null nodes, counting the null gaps between them as if the tree were complete. Return the largest width across all levels.
hints: BFS pairing each node with a positional index — left child = 2*i, right = 2*i+1.
At each level, width = lastIdx - firstIdx + 1.
Reset indices per level (subtract first idx) to avoid overflow.

title: Longest ZigZag Path in a Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (using `null` for missing nodes), return the length of the longest zigzag path. A zigzag path alternates direction (left, right, left, ...) at each step, and its length is measured in edges.
hints: DFS returning two values per node: longest zigzag path starting here going LEFT first, going RIGHT first.
If I start by going left, my next step is determined by my left child's right-zig.
Track global max separately.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree II
description: Given a binary tree provided as a level-order encoded array `data` (using `null` for missing nodes), build the tree, serialize it to a comma-separated preorder string using `#` to mark null nodes (e.g. `1,2,#,#,3,#,#`), deserialize that string back into a tree, and return the resulting tree as a level-order encoded array.
hints: Serialize: preorder DFS, append node.val (or '#') and a comma.
Deserialize: split on commas, consume tokens via an index pointer.
Round-trip should reproduce the canonical level-order array.

title: Maximum Leaf to Leaf Path Sum
description: Given a binary tree encoded in level-order as `tree` (node values may be negative), return the maximum sum among all leaf-to-leaf paths. Such a path starts and ends at two distinct leaves and may pass through any intermediate nodes. If the tree has fewer than two leaves, return -10000 as a sentinel value.
hints: DFS returning the best root-to-leaf chain sum from a node downward.
At a node with TWO non-null children, candidate = leftBest + rightBest + node.val.
If only one child exists, propagate but don't update the global best yet.
